title: Functions & Transformations
blurb: Function notation, domain and range, shifts, stretches, reflections — and inverse functions that undo it all.

lessons.title: Function Notation
lessons.concreteComparison: A vending machine: press B4, get the juice. Same button, same juice — every time, exactly one output. That's a **function**, and $f(x)$ is its receipt language: $f(\text{B4}) = \text{juice}$. Algebra's version: $f(x) = 2x + 1$ is a machine, $f(3)$ is 'press 3', and the display reads $7$.
lessons.objective: Read and evaluate function notation $f(x)$, including expression inputs like $f(a+2)$ and chained evaluations.
concept: A **function** assigns each input exactly ONE output. Notation: $f(x) = 2x + 1$ names the machine $f$ and its rule; $f(3)$ means 'run the machine on 3': $f(3) = 2(3)+1 = 7$.
$f(x)$ is NOT '$f$ times $x$' — it's 'the machine $f$ applied to $x$'. The parentheses are a doorway, not multiplication.
Anything can walk through the doorway: $f(-2) = -3$, $f(a) = 2a+1$, $f(a+2) = 2(a+2)+1 = 2a+5$ — substitute the WHOLE input everywhere $x$ appears.
Machines chain: if $g(x) = x^2$, then $f(g(2)) = f(4) = 9$ — inner machine first, its output feeds the outer one.
lessons.keyIdea: f(x) = the machine f run on input x — one output per input; substitute the whole input everywhere x appears.

facts.title: One rule
facts.explanation: That's what makes it a function.

workedExamples.statement: For $f(x) = x^2 - 3x$: find $f(5)$ and $f(a+1)$ (check your formula at $a = 4$).

commonMistakes.correction: $f(x)$ is machine-on-input. If $f(x) = x^2$, then $f(3) = 9$ — not '3 times f'. The parentheses here never mean multiply.

commonMistakes.text: Substituting an expression input into only ONE of the x's.
commonMistakes.correction: $f(x) = x^2 - 3x$ at $a+1$: BOTH x's get the whole input — $(a+1)^2 - 3(a+1)$. Missing the second one is the classic slip.

steps.title: One button, one snack
beats: A function is a vending machine: input in, output out.
Same input ALWAYS gives the same output. One each.

steps.title: Not multiplication
beats: The parentheses are a DOORWAY for the input.
Output has synonyms: $y$, $f(x)$, 'the value'. One idea.

steps.title: Machine operations
steps.intro: Every x gets the whole input; chains run inside-out.

problems.prompt: Which table can NOT be a function?
problems.explanation: One input with TWO outputs breaks the rule. (Two inputs sharing an output is fine — machines may repeat snacks.)

steps.title: The 300-year-old notation
steps.body: The $f(x)$ notation was introduced by Leonhard Euler in 1734 — and it stuck so hard that every programming language you'll ever use copies it: Python's f(x), a spreadsheet's SUM(A1:A9), even your calculator's sin(30). When you call a function in code, you're writing 18th-century Swiss mathematics.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A function gives each input exactly one output.
$f(3)$ = run machine $f$ on 3; parentheses are a doorway.
Expression inputs replace EVERY $x$; chains run inside-out.

lessons.title: Domain & Range
lessons.concreteComparison: A parking garage's ticket machine takes cars — not trucks (too tall), not bicycles (too light for the sensor). Every machine has a list of inputs it accepts: its **domain**. And the set of outputs it can produce — floors 1 through 5 — is its **range**. Function machines are the same: $\tfrac{1}{x-3}$ simply won't take $x = 3$.
lessons.objective: Find the domain of a function by excluding division-by-zero and negative-even-root inputs, and read off simple ranges.
concept: **Domain** = all legal inputs. **Range** = all possible outputs. For $f(x) = x^2$: domain is all reals (any number squares); range is $y \ge 0$ (squares are never negative).
Domain restriction 1: **no dividing by zero**. $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{x-3}$: domain is all reals EXCEPT $x = 3$. (Grade 10's excluded values — same idea, new vocabulary.)
Domain restriction 2: **no square roots of negatives**. $f(x) = \sqrt{x-2}$: need $x - 2 \ge 0$, so the domain is $x \ge 2$.
Ranges follow from the shape: $\sqrt{\ }$ outputs are $\ge 0$; $x^2 + 3$ outputs are $\ge 3$; a linear $2x+1$ hits every real. Ask: what CAN the display show?
lessons.keyIdea: Domain = legal inputs (no ÷0, no √negative). Range = achievable outputs. Read both from the rule's shape.

workedExamples.solution: All reals except $x = 3$ (the bottom dies there: $3 - 3 = 0$).
$x^2 \ge 0$, so $f(x) \ge 3$: range $y \ge 3$, with the minimum $3$ hit at $x=0$.

commonMistakes.text: Excluding values that zero the TOP of a fraction.
commonMistakes.correction: $f(x) = \tfrac{x-4}{x+1}$: only $x = -1$ is out. $x = 4$ just outputs $0$ — a legal snack.
$\ge$, not $>$: $\sqrt{0} = 0$ is perfectly fine. The root only rejects NEGATIVES.

steps.title: Domain and range
beats: Every machine has inputs it ACCEPTS: the **domain**.
And outputs it can PRODUCE: the **range**.

steps.title: No dividing by zero
beats: (Grade 10 called these excluded values. Same landmine.)

steps.title: Spot the jam
steps.explanation: Only the BOTTOM jams the machine: $x = 7$. ($x=-2$ outputs 0 — legal.)

steps.title: No roots of negatives
beats: Solve the little inequality; that's the domain.

steps.title: List the legal inputs
steps.intro: Hunt both landmines; then ask what the display can show.

problems.explanation: The rule divides by $x$, so $0$ jams it — even though it 'would simplify' to $x$. The machine runs AS WRITTEN.
$x + 7 \ge 0 \to x \ge -7$. Negative INPUTS are fine; negative INSIDES are not.
$x^2 \ge 0$, so the display never dips below $-4$ (hit at $x=0$).

steps.title: Domain first, always
steps.body: Before ANY work with a function — evaluating, transforming, inverting — sweep for the two landmines. Thirty seconds of domain-checking prevents the classic exam disaster: a beautiful answer at an input the function never accepted.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Domain = legal inputs; range = achievable outputs.
$\ge$ at the root's edge — $\sqrt{0} = 0$ is legal.

lessons.title: Shifts
lessons.concreteComparison: You already moved parabolas in Grade 10: $(x-3)^2 + 2$ slid the vertex to $(3, 2)$. The plot twist: that wasn't a parabola fact — it's a fact about EVERY function. $f(x) + k$ lifts ANY graph by $k$; $f(x-h)$ slides ANY graph right by $h$. One pair of rules, every curve you'll ever meet.
lessons.objective: Shift any function's graph with $f(x) + k$ (vertical) and $f(x-h)$ (horizontal), predicting points before plotting.
concept: **Vertical shift**: $g(x) = f(x) + k$ adds $k$ to every output — the whole graph rises by $k$ (drops if $k<0$). Outside the function = vertical.
**Horizontal shift**: $g(x) = f(x - h)$ slides the graph RIGHT by $h$. Inside the function = horizontal — and the sign is sneaky, same as vertex form: $(x-3)$ moves right, $(x+3)$ left.
Why inside flips direction: $g(5) = f(5-3) = f(2)$ — to output what $f$ showed at 2, $g$ waits until 5. The feature arrives LATER (further right).
Point tracking beats memorizing: if $(2, 4)$ is on $f$, then $f(x)+3$ has $(2, 7)$, and $f(x-1)$ has $(3, 4)$. Move the points; the curve follows.
lessons.keyIdea: Outside = vertical, moves as written; inside = horizontal, moves opposite the sign. Track a point to be sure.

facts.title: Horizontal
facts.explanation: Inside: the bracket's zero is the shift.

workedExamples.statement: $f(x) = x^2$. Where does the vertex land under $g(x) = f(x) + 3$ and $h(x) = f(x-4)$?
$(2, 5)$ sits on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = f(x) - 2$ and on $y = f(x+1)$.
Write $y = x^2$ shifted right 2 and up 5, and give one point on it.

commonMistakes.correction: Inside flips: $f(x+3)$ is LEFT 3 — its input reaches any value 3 steps early. Track a point: $g(-3) = f(0)$.
Outside the parentheses touches the OUTPUT → vertical. Inside touches the INPUT → horizontal. Location decides the direction.

tryIt.statement: $(4, 1)$ is on $y = f(x)$. Its image on $y = f(x) + 6$?
$(4, 1)$ is on $y = f(x)$. Its image on $y = f(x - 3)$?

steps.title: It was never about parabolas
beats: $(x-3)^2 + 2$: vertex slid to $(3, 2)$. You know this.
New headline: works for EVERY function $f$.
$f(x-h)$: whole graph right $h$. Any curve. Always.

steps.title: Slide and lift, live
steps.teach: The old friend, reread through function eyes: stepping $k$ computes $f(x) + k$ (outside → vertical), stepping $h$ computes $f(x-h)$ (inside → horizontal). Watch the equation update as the graph glides — the notation IS the movement.

steps.title: The waiting argument
beats: $g(5) = f(2)$ — the machine sees 2 when you're at 5.
Every feature of $f$ arrives 3 steps LATER. Later = right.

steps.title: Direction call
steps.explanation: Inside, plus → LEFT: $g(-5) = f(0)$, so the old start shows up 5 early.

steps.title: Follow (3, 8)
steps.prompt: $(3, 8)$ lies on $y = f(x)$. On $y = f(x-2) + 1$ its image is…
steps.explanation: Right 2: $x = 5$. Up 1: $y = 9$. Both shifts, one point.

steps.title: Point tracking
steps.intro: Inside → x moves (opposite); outside → y moves (as written).

examples.prompt: $f(x) = |x|$ has its corner at $(0,0)$. Where is the corner of $y = |x - 4| - 2$?
$g(x) = \sqrt{x-1} + 3$: where does the graph START, and what's $g(5)$?

steps.title: Shift-craft
steps.intro: Location decides direction; sign inside flips.

problems.explanation: Outside the squaring: outputs all gain 7. Vertical.

steps.title: Your phone shifts functions all day
steps.body: Drag a photo across the screen and the graphics chip computes $image(x - h, y - k)$ for every pixel — a horizontal-and-vertical shift, millions of times a second. Scrolling, panning a map, moving a game character: all of it is $f(x-h)+k$ running on silicon. You just learned the math your thumb has been doing for years.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $f(x) + k$: vertical, honest sign. $f(x-h)$: horizontal, flipped sign.
Inside touches input (x moves); outside touches output (y moves).
Track one point through the moves — the curve follows.

lessons.title: Stretches & Reflections
lessons.concreteComparison: A funhouse mirror hall: one mirror makes you twice as tall, one squashes you to half height, one flips you upside down, one swaps left and right. Graphs visit the same hall: $2f(x)$ doubles every height, $\tfrac12 f(x)$ halves them, $-f(x)$ flips the graph over the x-axis, and $f(-x)$ mirrors it left-right.
lessons.objective: Stretch, squash, and reflect graphs with $a \cdot f(x)$ and $f(-x)$, tracking points through each move.
concept: **Vertical stretch**: $g(x) = a \cdot f(x)$ multiplies every OUTPUT by $a$. $|a| > 1$ stretches tall; $0 < |a| < 1$ squashes flat. $(2, 4)$ on $f$ → $(2, 12)$ on $3f$.
**Flip down**: $-f(x)$ negates outputs — reflection over the x-axis. Peaks become valleys. (Grade 10's frowning parabolas: $-x^2$ was this all along.)
**Mirror sideways**: $f(-x)$ negates INPUTS — reflection over the y-axis. $(2, 4)$ on $f$ → $(-2, 4)$ on $f(-x)$.

Special graphs shrug: $x^2$ under $f(-x)$ is unchanged ($(-x)^2 = x^2$) — it was already symmetric (an **even** function). $x^3$ satisfies $f(-x) = -f(x)$ — flipping sideways = flipping down (an **odd** function).
lessons.keyIdea: a·f(x): outputs ×a (stretch/squash/flip-down when negative). f(−x): mirror over the y-axis. Points tell the story.

facts.title: Two mirrors
facts.explanation: Minus outside flips down; inside flips sideways.

workedExamples.statement: $(3, 2)$ is on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = 4f(x)$ and $y = \tfrac12 f(x)$.
$(3, 2)$ is on $y = f(x)$. Find its image on $y = -f(x)$ and $y = f(-x)$.
Show $f(x) = x^2$ is even and $g(x) = x^3$ is odd (test at $x = 2$).

commonMistakes.text: Scaling x too: sending $(3, 2)$ to $(6, 4)$ under $2f(x)$.
commonMistakes.correction: $a f(x)$ touches OUTPUTS only: $(3, 4)$. The graph grows taller, not wider — inputs never move.
$-f(x)$: minus OUTSIDE → flip over the x-axis (upside down). $f(-x)$: minus INSIDE → flip over the y-axis (left-right). Inside touches input, outside touches output — same law as shifts.

steps.title: Four mirrors
beats: $2f(x)$: twice as tall. $\tfrac12 f(x)$: half height.
All four: multiply an input or an output by something.

steps.title: The a dial, revisited
steps.teach: Grade 10's $a$ dial was this lesson in disguise: stepping $a$ computes $a \cdot f(x)$ for $f(x) = x^2$ — taller past $|a| = 1$, flatter below it, and the flip at negative $a$ is exactly $-f(x)$, the x-axis reflection. One dial, three transformations.

steps.title: Which mirror?
beats: $-f(x)$: the minus hits the OUTPUT. Peaks → valleys.
$f(-x)$: the minus hits the INPUT. Left ↔ right.
Same inside/outside law as shifts. It never changes.

steps.title: Name the flip
steps.prompt: The graph of $y = f(-x)$ is $f$ reflected over…
steps.explanation: Minus inside = input negated = left-right mirror over the y-axis.

steps.title: Mirror math
steps.intro: Outputs for outside; inputs for inside.

examples.prompt: Is $h(x) = x^4 - 3x^2$ even, odd, or neither? (Test $x = 2$.)
steps: Even — all even powers. Symmetric over the y-axis.

steps.title: Hall of mirrors
steps.intro: Track the point; trust the floors.

problems.explanation: Every output ×5: a vertical stretch. (Up 5 would be $f(x) + 5$.)
Both mirrors: sideways ($x \to -1$) and down ($y \to -7$). For odd functions this lands back ON the graph!

problems.prompt: Which function equals its own y-axis mirror ($f(-x) = f(x)$)?

steps.title: Even and odd run physics
steps.body: Physicists sort the universe's functions by mirror behavior: cosine is even (rewinding time looks identical), sine is odd (rewinding flips the wave). Electron orbitals, crystal vibrations, quantum selection rules — half of theoretical physics starts by asking this lesson's question: what happens under $f(-x)$?

steps.title: What you learned
points: $a f(x)$: outputs ×a — taller, flatter, or flipped (a < 0).
$-f(x)$ flips over the x-axis; $f(-x)$ mirrors over the y-axis.

lessons.title: Combining Transformations
lessons.concreteComparison: A recipe with steps out of order ruins the dish — season THEN sear beats sear THEN season. Transformations stack the same way: $y = 2f(x - 3) + 1$ is slide right 3, stretch ×2, lift 1 — and doing the lift before the stretch would double the wrong thing. One master form, one safe order.
lessons.objective: Read and build the full form $y = a\,f(x-h) + k$, applying transformations in a correct order and tracking points through the whole stack.
concept: The master form: $y = a \cdot f(x-h) + k$ — stretch/flip by $a$, slide right $h$, lift $k$. Every graph this year fits it.
Safe order: **inside first** (horizontal shift), then **scale** ($\times a$), then **outside shift** ($+k$). It mirrors order of operations on the formula itself.
Why order matters: take $(0,1)$ under 'stretch ×2 then up 1' → $(0, 3)$. Under 'up 1 then stretch ×2' → $(0, 4)$. Different! The formula $2f(x)+1$ says: stretch first.
Point-track the stack: $(1, 4)$ on $f$ under $y = 2f(x-3) + 1$: right 3 → $(4, 4)$; ×2 → $(4, 8)$; up 1 → $(4, 9)$.
lessons.keyIdea: y = a·f(x−h) + k: slide (h), scale (a), lift (k) — in that order. Track one point through the whole stack.

workedExamples.statement: Describe $y = 2(x-3)^2 + 1$ as transformations of $y = x^2$, and give the vertex.
workedExamples.solution: Right 3, stretch ×2, up 1. Vertex $(3, 1)$. Check: at $x=4$: $2(1)+1 = 3$.

workedExamples.statement: Write $\sqrt{x}$ flipped over the x-axis, shifted left 2 and up 5, then evaluate at $x = 2$.

commonMistakes.correction: $2f(x) + 1$ stretches FIRST, then lifts: outputs go $y \to 2y \to 2y+1$. Pre-lifting gives $2y + 2$ — a different graph. Follow the formula's order of operations.

commonMistakes.text: Describing $2f(x-3)+1$ as 'right 3, up 1, stretch 2' and stretching the +1.
commonMistakes.correction: The k rides OUTSIDE the stretch — it's applied last and never doubles. Vertex $(3, 1)$, not $(3, 2)$.

steps.title: All three dials
steps.teach: Standard-form features live under the same master form: stepping $b$ and $c$ reshapes $ax^2+bx+c$, and the axis, vertex, and intercept chase the formula. Every quadratic you graphed in Grade 10 was a transformation stack of $x^2$ all along.

steps.title: Read the stack
steps.explanation: Inside $(x+4)$: LEFT 4. Then ×½, then down 3 — the $-3$ is outside the squash and never halves.

steps.title: Stack running
steps.intro: Inside → scale → lift, one point at a time.

examples.prompt: Describe $y = -2\sqrt{x-1} + 6$ and find where it starts.
Build: $|x|$ stretched ×3, right 2, down 5. Then evaluate at $x = 4$.

steps.title: Full-form fluency
steps.intro: Name the moves; run one point.

problems.explanation: Right 5, up 2; the flip makes it a frown — vertex on top.

problems.prompt: Which equation is $\sqrt{x}$ shifted left 3 and squashed to half height?

steps.title: Every video game is a transformation stack
steps.body: A game engine draws each character as ONE master model run through $a \cdot f(x-h) + k$ thousands of times: scaled for distance, slid to position, flipped when facing left. Animators call it a transform matrix; you now know its algebra. The boss monster is just $f$; the whole level is the stack.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Master form $y = a f(x-h) + k$: slide, scale, lift — that order.
The $k$ rides outside the stretch and never scales.
Track one point through the stack to verify any description.

lessons.title: Inverse Functions
lessons.concreteComparison: Every path out is a path back: zip a file, unzip it; convert °C to °F, convert back. An **inverse function** $f^{-1}$ is the machine that UNDOES $f$: if $f$ turns 5 into 13, $f^{-1}$ turns 13 into 5. Finding it is a two-step dance — swap, then solve.
lessons.objective: Find inverses of linear (and simple root/quadratic-piece) functions by swap-and-solve, verify by composition, and read the y = x mirror.
concept: $f^{-1}$ undoes $f$: $f(5) = 13$ means $f^{-1}(13) = 5$. Inputs and outputs trade jobs — the domain and range swap.
Recipe: write $y = f(x)$; **swap** $x$ and $y$; **solve** for $y$. For $f(x) = 2x + 3$: $x = 2y + 3 \to y = \tfrac{x-3}{2}$. So $f^{-1}(x) = \tfrac{x-3}{2}$.
The verification handshake: $f^{-1}(f(x)) = x$ AND $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$ — running the machine then its undo returns the original. Check: $f(5) = 13$, $f^{-1}(13) = 5$ ✓.

Graphs: $y = f^{-1}(x)$ is $y = f(x)$ reflected over the line $y = x$ — every point $(a, b)$ crosses to $(b, a)$. And undoing is order-reversed: $f(x) = 2x + 3$ multiplies then adds, so $f^{-1}$ subtracts then divides — socks-and-shoes.
lessons.keyIdea: f⁻¹ undoes f: swap x and y, solve for y. Verify with f(f⁻¹(x)) = x; the graphs mirror across y = x.

commonMistakes.correction: The $-1$ is UNDO, not reciprocal. For $f(x) = 2x+3$: $f^{-1}(13) = 5$, while $\tfrac{1}{f(13)} = \tfrac{1}{29}$. Completely different animals.

commonMistakes.text: Undoing operations in the same order they were applied.
commonMistakes.correction: $f$: multiply by 2, THEN add 3. $f^{-1}$: subtract 3 FIRST, then divide — reverse order, like shoes off before socks.

steps.title: Undo it
steps.explanation: Adding 9 is undone by subtracting 9. (The reciprocal is the classic $-1$ misread.)

steps.title: Reverse the order
beats: Undo in reverse order — shoes off before socks.

steps.title: Verify by composition
steps.explanation: $f(3) = 4$, $g(4) = 3$ — back where we started. $g(f(x)) = x$ is the inverse handshake.

examples.prompt: Celsius to Fahrenheit is $F = \tfrac95 C + 32$. Invert it and convert $86°F$.
$f(x) = \sqrt{x - 4}$: find $f^{-1}$ and its rule's domain note.

problems.explanation: Times 5 undoes with divide by 5. Check: $f(3)=15$, $\tfrac{15}{5}=3$ ✓.
Inverses trade jobs: output 11 came from input 2 — no formula needed.

problems.prompt: The graph of $f^{-1}$ is the graph of $f$ reflected over…
problems.explanation: Each point $(a,b)$ swaps to $(b,a)$ — the mirror that swaps coordinates is $y = x$.

steps.title: Encryption is a one-way function
steps.body: Internet security rests on functions that are easy to run and brutally hard to UNDO: multiplying two 300-digit primes takes microseconds, but inverting it (factoring the product) would take the best computers longer than the universe's age. Your bank password is protected by an inverse that exists — but no one can compute. This lesson's swap-and-solve is the easy case; cryptographers weaponize the hard one.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Verify: $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$ both ways — the handshake.
Graphs mirror across $y = x$; undo steps run in reverse order.

practice.statement: $f(x) = x^2 - 2x$: find $f(a+1)$ in expanded form (check at $a=3$).
Is $f(x) = x^3 - x$ even, odd, or neither? (Test $x = 2$.)

testYourself.statement: Which mirror: $y = f(-x)$ vs $y = -f(x)$ — where does $(3, 7)$ land under each?
Write $\sqrt{x}$ shifted right 4, stretched ×2, down 1 — and evaluate at $x = 13$.
